title: Quality Management
summary: How to plan, manage and control quality so deliverables conform to requirements and meet customer needs. In PMBOK 8 this maps to the Embed Quality Into Processes and Deliverables principle, with quality assurance sitting in the Governance domain and scope-level quality in the Scope domain.

sections.heading: Embed Quality principle, Scope and Governance domains
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle- and outcome-based. Quality maps to the Embed Quality Into Processes and Deliverables principle - build quality in, do not inspect it out. Quality assurance sits in the Governance domain, while scope-level quality (deliverables meeting scope and acceptance criteria) lives in the Scope domain.

blocks.title: How quality shows up
lines: Embed Quality principle: design quality in, do not inspect it out.
Governance domain: quality assurance and audits govern the processes.
Scope domain: deliverables meet scope and acceptance criteria.
Prevention over inspection is the founding idea behind every choice.

sections.heading: Quality management order
sections.body: Standards are set first, processes are assured next, deliverables are inspected, and only then does the customer accept. Sequence matters: deliverables are verified internally in Control Quality before being taken to the customer for validated acceptance in Validate Scope.

items.title: Plan Quality
lines: Set the standards, metrics and required level of accuracy and precision.

items.title: Manage Quality
lines: Process-focused and preventive: audits, process, prevention.
Ask 'are we following the right processes that will produce good deliverables?'

items.title: Control Quality
lines: Product-focused and inspection-based: inspect deliverables for defects against the spec.
Ask 'is this specific deliverable correct?'

items.title: Validate Scope
lines: Customer or sponsor reviews completed deliverables and formally accepts them.

blocks.title: Exam cues
lines: Audit -> Manage Quality (process, prevention).
Inspect a deliverable -> Control Quality (product).
Customer sign-off -> Validate Scope (acceptance).
Control Quality before Validate Scope.

sections.heading: Cost of Quality (CoQ)
sections.body: Cost of Quality is the total cost of all effort spent to achieve quality across the product's life. It splits into two families: conformance costs (money spent to avoid defects) and non-conformance costs (money spent because of defects).

groups.label: Prevention
lines: Training, documenting processes, equipment, time to do it right.

groups.label: Appraisal
lines: Testing, inspection, audits.

groups.label: Internal failure
lines: Rework and scrap found before the customer sees it.

groups.label: External failure
lines: Warranty work, liabilities, lost reputation found after delivery.

blocks.title: Core message
lines: Cost of conformance = prevention + appraisal.
Cost of non-conformance = internal + external failure.
Prevention is almost always cheaper than failure, and the cost of fixing a defect rises sharply the later it is found. Prevention buys down failure.

sections.heading: Quality assurance vs quality control
sections.body: A simple memory aid: assurance is about doing the right things (process), control is about checking the things are right (product). Audits belong to assurance; inspections belong to control.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Process-focused and preventive.

groups.label: Asks
lines: Are we following the right processes that will produce good deliverables?

groups.label: Main tool
lines: The audit - examines whether processes and procedures are applied correctly.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Product-focused and inspection-based.

groups.label: Asks
lines: Is this specific deliverable correct?

groups.label: Main tool
lines: Inspection - measures or examines the actual output against requirements.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: QA = process, prevention, audits.
QC = product, inspection.
Audit a process, inspect a deliverable.

sections.heading: The 7 basic quality tools
sections.body: The classic seven tools used mainly in Control Quality to collect data, find root causes and judge whether a process is behaving.

rows.value: Ishikawa / fishbone; traces a problem back to its root causes.
Maps the process steps to expose where defects can arise.
Tally sheet to collect defect data during inspection.
Ranks defects to reveal the vital few (80/20) causes.
Bar chart of how often each value or defect occurs.
Tracks process stability against control limits over time.
Plots two variables to reveal correlation between them.

sections.heading: Prevention over inspection, and sampling
sections.body: A founding principle of modern quality is that prevention is preferable to inspection. It is better to design quality in than to inspect defects out after they occur. When you do measure, two sampling approaches apply.

rows.value: Pass or fail: the result either conforms or it does not (counts).
Measures a result on a continuous scale (length, weight, time), giving a degree of conformity.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Attribute = pass/fail (counts).
Variable = measured on a scale (degrees).

sections.heading: Control limits, specification limits and the Rule of Seven
sections.body:
On a control chart, control limits represent the voice of the process: the range of natural variation the process produces when it is stable. They are calculated from the process data, usually at plus or minus three standard deviations.

Specification limits represent the voice of the customer: the boundaries the customer or requirements allow. They sit outside the control chart and are not derived from the process. A capable process keeps its control limits comfortably inside the specification limits.

rows.value: Voice of the process; natural variation, usually +/- 3 standard deviations, calculated from the data.
Voice of the customer; the boundaries requirements allow, set outside the process.

blocks.title: Rule of Seven
lines: If seven consecutive points fall on one side of the mean (or trend in one direction), investigate the process even though no single point breached a control limit.
Such a run is statistically unlikely by chance and signals a non-random, assignable cause.

sections.heading: Quality vs grade, accuracy vs precision, verification vs validation
sections.body: Three pairs the exam loves to blur.

groups.label: Quality
lines: Degree to which a deliverable conforms to its requirements.

groups.label: Grade
lines: A category for deliverables with the same functional use but different technical characteristics.

groups.label: Rule
lines: Low grade can be acceptable if agreed; low quality never is.
A basic phone (low grade) with no defects is high quality; a feature-rich phone (high grade) that keeps crashing is low quality.

groups.label: Accuracy
lines: How close a measured value is to the true or target value.

groups.label: Precision
lines: How close repeated measurements are to one another, regardless of the target.

groups.label: Rule
lines: Independent properties: you can be precise but off-target, or scattered but averaging onto the bullseye.
Decide the required level of each in quality planning; chasing more than needed adds cost.

groups.label: Verification
lines: 'Did we build it right?' Internal check that a deliverable conforms to its spec, standards and regulations.
Maps to Control Quality (inspect against the spec).

groups.label: Validation
lines: 'Did we build the right thing?' External check that the deliverable meets the customer's needs and is fit for purpose.
Maps to Validate Scope (customer formally accepts).

groups.label: Sequence
lines: Verify internally in Control Quality before validated acceptance in Validate Scope.

blocks.title: Exam cues
lines: Low grade can be acceptable; low quality never is.
Accuracy = on target; precision = tightly grouped - they are separate.
Verification = built right (internal, spec); validation = built the right thing (customer acceptance).
Inspect = Control Quality; sign-off = Validate Scope.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and restructured for the PMBOK 8 principle- and domain-based PMP exam.
